claimed_contribution: The paper distinguishes internal and external motivations for unplanned purchasing and links buying impulsivity to initial motivational salience, helping reconcile prior mixed findings on impulsivity and total unplanned purchasing.
It provides direct evidence that unplanned-purchase motivations change within a trip in patterns consistent with motivation balancing, with budget focus as a boundary condition.

It demonstrates behavioral and managerial implications by showing dynamic fit effects for point-of-purchase messages and predictable within-trip differences in relative spending on unplanned purchases.
context: The studies are set in grocery shopping, including real in-store trips in two grocery stores and incentive-compatible online grocery shopping tasks. The focal behavior is category- and brand-level unplanned purchasing during a single shopping trip.
design_and_data: Evidence comes from a multimethod program with two field studies and five online experiments. Study 1a used head-mounted video tracking plus entry and exit surveys for 250 grocery shoppers (237 with complete video), and coded motivations for 277 unplanned purchases with reported reasons; Study 1b used in-store point-of-purchase intercepts from 108 shoppers (79 unplanned purchases). Online studies with student and Prolific samples randomized category order and motivation measurement timing, manipulated trip budget or budget focus, and measured internal versus external motivations using open-ended coding or scales. Additional experiments manipulated internal versus external point-of-purchase messages to model nonlist purchase conversion, and varied within-category price options to test relative spending patterns.
main_findings: At the beginning of a shopping trip, higher buying impulsivity increased the likelihood that an unplanned purchase was internally rather than externally motivated.

As cumulative unplanned purchase consideration increased, high-impulsivity shoppers shifted away from internal motivations while low-impulsivity shoppers shifted toward internal motivations, and this dynamic weakened under higher budget focus.

Behavior tracked these dynamics: internal versus external point-of-purchase message effectiveness changed with impulsivity and trip progress, and relative spending on unplanned purchases decreased with progress for high-impulsivity shoppers but increased for low-impulsivity shoppers.
one_line_summary: Across two grocery field studies and five online shopping experiments, the paper shows that buying impulsivity shapes whether unplanned purchases are initially internally or externally motivated and how those motivations shift during the trip, with consequences for message effectiveness and spending.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior research on unplanned purchasing and sequential choice by distinguishing internal versus external motivations, modeling their within-trip shifts, and linking those shifts to message response and relative spending.
research_gap: Prior unplanned-purchasing research often treated unplanned purchases as homogeneous and did not distinguish underlying motivations. The paper also identifies a lack of evidence on when and why motivations change within a trip and whether these motivation patterns alter in-store marketing effectiveness and spending behavior.
research_question: How do internal versus external motivations for unplanned purchases vary across a shopping trip? The paper tests whether buying impulsivity predicts initial motivational salience and whether cumulative unplanned purchase consideration shifts motivations through motivation balancing. It also examines whether these dynamics influence point-of-purchase message effectiveness and relative spending on unplanned purchases.
